payload.title: The framing paradox: more evidence creates more skepticism
payload.body:
I articulated something today that I have been feeling but never fully stated. The catch-22: I am an excellent candidate for a multitude of PM jobs — fraud, auth, payments, digital experiences, mobile, AI, growth and adoption. But showing all of that range simultaneously activates the exact biases that work against me.

Four biases compound: banking stigma (Wells Fargo = slow enterprise), seniority bias (companies shedding managers, wanting ICs), breadth skepticism (too many domains = no depth in any), and volume problem (30+ initiatives triggers disbelief that one person did it all). Each is manageable alone. Together they create a situation where the more complete my career page is, the more ammunition it gives skeptics.

The hard truth: I was a workaholic. My individual output was 5x the rest of the team combined at Wells. I have been the best performer on every team by a fair distance. But for an outsider looking in, that story is literally unbelievable. And the system does not reward people who did more — it rewards people who tell a clean narrow story.

The answer is not to fight these biases directly on the page. It is to design around them — use capability zones instead of timelines, abstract company names, let the site itself be the depth proof.
payload.decision: Design around the biases rather than fighting them head-on. The page structure itself is the counter-argument.
payload.alternatives_considered: Gating approach (let people self-identify, show curated lens). Showing everything (current approach).
payload.open_questions: How to handle the Wells Fargo return in the narrative. How to position consulting work. How to future-proof for whichever PM specialization is most in demand 6-18 months from now.
